sentences.text: See, as the people came across the Niesse River, as I say, they were trying to find a hole to sit down, to lay down, whatever.
The gendarmes did not give them peace, because, they-- first, some people, if they had any money, they were looking for money.
So if you can give them something in the hand, they let you.
But most of the people-- what money?
Where money?
Should I put the suitcase [INAUDIBLE]?
So they just had them march up the hill.
And on top of the hill were ravines, like, cliffs.
And they take everything.
You'd have, a little suitcase, or a little something hanging.
They thought, who knows?
They [? schlepped ?] [? everything ?] away and threw them into the ravines.
Oh, they were a mass.
A mass was there.
And they did it because they didn't want to keep too many in the ghetto-- Mogilev-Podolski, because it would break out a big epidemic.
It did. 1941, within a couple of months, broke out Fleck typhus, which they called from the lice, because you didn't-- you know, there were-- you were dressed, and you couldn't wash.
You could nothing there.
So as a matter of fact, it's funny.
When I was on this side of the Niesse, before they send us across, in the first night, it was cold already.
I had a sweater on.
I remember like today.
And I took off the sweater, but you were near the river, so it could still go [INAUDIBLE].
And for the first time, I saw a big lice, like, you know.
And I asked my parents.
Well, that's the way.
But then-- and then the lice bites you, and you get that fever.
And you-- it goes into your head-- the high temperature-- no aspirin, nothing.
And you burn up.
So that winter, I would say 90,000 minimum, what I understand, die of this epidemic.
They took them up.
The Germans and the Romanians were there, you know?
They were afraid epidemic.
They took them up.
It so happened, it must have been, once, a big Jewish community, Mogilev.
There was a big Jewish cemetery-- big kind of hill, like, 10 kilometer out of the way.
That was the Ukraine-- Russia-- occupation by the German and Romanian.
And there were-- as I said, they took these people up.
They had to put dynamite because it was-- everything was solid, frozen-- very bad winter, 1941.
And they-- mass grave-- put them all in and cover up.
And of course, I had-- I had typhoid fever, which goes into your stomach.
And that also-- they isolate you, and it goes down in your legs, and you cannot walk.
You're very sick.
But they put you in the hospital, you know, not much, or something like this.
My father died there, too, because of that, OK?
Were you given food and water?
No.
In the hospital, yeah.
Food-- if what you want to call food.
If you got a little bit, a piece of bread with some water in the hospital, you did.
And I tell you, also, why.
Because those were civilian.
That was no military hospital.
Was the Ukraine population, you know?
Nothing happened to them.
You follow me?
As a matter of fact, they got, in a way, rich, because anything they could-- if you want a potato, one potato-- I'll just bring it up in American dollar, maybe-- you want a potato?
Pay $5.
If not, starve.
Where you get $5, you know?
I'm just saying.
So they took advantage.
Whatever they came out, they used to sit, used to be a little place there where people used to change.
You know, what they call here in America free market or something like this?
[INAUDIBLE] change.
What do you got?
You still have a little ring?
OK.
I'm going give you one pound of corn meal.
So you will have, for two meals, for a little diamond ring, which may be worth two, three, whatever.
How did your parents manage with the food?
Well, this is the way how you had to manage-- the best you can.
And you had to eat, what you call, you get an entree, and you get a first course, the second, the third, like you go to a restaurant.
The first course is potato, then the second entree is potato soup, and dessert is another potato.
So you get three ways of this.
So this is how you man-- you imagine things.
You feel good.
Try a little potato puree, a little mashed potatoes, a little water for the potatoes is potato soup.
Forget about if you think of any meat or something.
Don't even think about.
Yeah.
They were from all over.
They were from Hungary, from Austria, from Yugoslavia, and of course a lot of Russian Jews, Ukrainian Jews.
They're all included.
However, see, they live over there I don't know how many years, you know, maybe 20, 30, 40, 50.
They were-- Jews, the community as such, they knew how to get around things, because they knew the peasants of the Ukraines.
You follow me?
Like, I remember, then, we finally moved into a Jew barber.
He was a barber.
That is funny.
He had maybe a 9' by 12' little room.
He let us in to sleep next to each other on the floor, all right?
Up front-- the front-- you know, he had the front.
He was a barber.
The doors were open.
Nobody knew he was Jewish.
And the Germans and the Romanian came for a shave and a haircut, and we were right there.
If they would know we are right behind, he would be shot and we-- probably send us up the ravine also.
[INAUDIBLE] this were for the time being, for the time we lived there.
And then, you had to move, quick, out, real fast to find another place.
Because if they smell, that they have-- so you change places, otherwise they pick you up, and they sent you out.
You know, they sent you on top to other places, and over the ravines, and you'll never survive.
So you-- you find another place.
You bribe what they called [RUSSIAN].
You bribe a [RUSSIAN].
[RUSSIAN] means a landlord in Russian.
Now she was-- I said she, because she was happy to get something, but you-- you know, they were poor over there anyway, you know?
So if you had something still, maybe a little fur, or a little shawl, or a little [INAUDIBLE], you gave her.
She'll let you just live there, because her husband was drafted already by the Russian army somewhere-- who knows-- on the battle front.
So she was glad to get something from you so she could put something on over there.
That's it.
Were there are walls around this area?
Yeah.
But bombed out.
If it was a wall, there was-- you could see direct, the sky.
You know, you had-- you all the time were air-conditioned.
Of course, I say that sarcastic.
Escape where?
Do you have-- If you can swim across the Mississippi River, go right.
You'll never make it anyway.

Now, the Romanian used to schlep the young kids, again, with a pontoon, you know, across the river on the other side when a big snow came to shovel the snow, to cut wood for their officer, because everything was heated over there.
It wasn't electric or gas, you know?
You had to have wood to clean-- to heat and to cook, or whatever.
So you had to go somewhere, cut wood.
I remember, like today, my father was [INAUDIBLE] I'm going to get killed by that, because they use to catch you when you go out the door.
They see a young man, uh huh.
They grab you by the head.
Come on.
They schlep to an officer.
He was a Romanian or a German officer.
You go and cut the wood.
And my father, right away, ran out, save the son.
No, I want to cut the wood for my son.
Let my son go.
So they-- they pushed him on the side, and they called him a dirty dog.
And I said to my father, don't worry about it.
I survive.
I'm still here, proof of the pudding.
I'm here.
I survived.
I cut wood.
It's all right.
And then, some of the officer were very nice.
Give me a piece of bread.
You know, they give me a piece of bread.
I brought it home.
That's OK, too.
But I wasn't worried about that.
The bad things came after that.
My father died.
I [INAUDIBLE] this.
Well, my father got that Fleck typhus, what they call, from the lice.
Now, I-- my mother slept separate in a children bed, you know-- in a baby crib.
And she had to hang over her feet to open the baby crib, and there was a little box so she can hold her legs on the box.
That's how she slept, in a baby crib.
Of course, you know?
And me and my father slept what they called in a regular steel bed, but no mattress, you know?
And I don't know if you ever saw these.
These have these, oh God, springs, but not mattress.
You know, these steel beds, they have springs?
And that's how you sleep.
Sure, you wake up, you have wounds, but you sleep.
And one night, in the morning, we wake up, and my mother start crying, pulling her hair, oy gevalt.
Dad is-- my father is dead, OK?
So it was a big winter.
So there was two ways.
Either let-- also let him up on the cemetery and will be buried in the mass grave, like they blow up with dynamite, eh?
Or do something about, and do just that what you could do.
Do it on your-- no funeral, naturally.
There was no funeral director, or funeral, or a casket, or so on.
Forget about that.
That didn't exist.
But my mother had a [NON-ENGLISH] which was a very expensive fur coat.
And she said, here it is.
I want to bury my husband.
So they organize that.
So first, they-- they brought-- I call it a troika-- one of these flat, little carriage with the Russian.
You see them sometime in the movie.
They are flat, and a big horse with a thing.
Like If you watched the movie, you saw these flat, little things.
And you put him on there, no cloth, nothing.
You cover him up with the tallits.
And you know, it's a bad road up the hill.
It's a long way to Tipperary, so to speak, because it's about 10 kilometer, again, up to the Jewish cemetery, right?
I go, and my mother goes, in the deep snow, schlepping along.
Took a long time-- no funeral, nothing.
They didn't even [INAUDIBLE] if father was still alive here.
Nobody know, you know.
The uncles weren't there, around, nothing.
Next of kin-- yeah, if I have another brother, so, probably would go.
Your mother and this-- that's all you were allowed.
The legs, covered this, and the legs are hanging over, and they shake.
Never forget this.
This is a terrible picture to see, you know, a person who's dead that's shaking like this.
Coming up, there were two more peasants with, you know-- with anything they had, because it was all frozen.
So you couldn't [INAUDIBLE].
So they brought heavy stuff, and shovels with the-- you know, the old [INAUDIBLE] graves, helped me put my father in, covered him with the tallits again, and that's it.
And then, again, still, we organized.
Somehow, my mother couldn't care, I'm sure.
That [INAUDIBLE] is.
That's why she survived, maybe also too.
But she gave away, again, whatever she had, and there was a stone.
And we made him a real matzevah, a real stone, in Hebrew.
I mean, it's there.
I've never been back, naturally, since 1941.
But it's supposed to be on the cemetery with the name carved, Bronstein, Hebrew, the date of when he died, and everything.
That's how I buried my father.
And then we lived the best we could, and Clipper start helping out.
I sent Nathan Clipper-- sent messengers,-- not he, through that general which sent soldiers in.
You know, soldiers could go in and out, right?

You know, and they came down to the community, and it was arranged that, not direct to the family branch then, because it was his father, and his mother, and the father, and the mother, and the brothers-- it was big families.
So the Clippers and the Bronsteins.
But it was so done that-- you know, it was also what they call a Jewish-- you cannot call it Jewish federation.
It had to be something organized there in that ghetto.
So they brought in whatever they could over there, and then it was the best distribute to anybody who need it.
Yeah.
We were aware of when it was-- oh, yeah-- when it was [NON-ENGLISH] and so, then, we had-- like, Passover, we had a big Passover-- double ration of potatoes.
And again, we were a few kids.
We knew where potatoes in, you know, in the [? summer ?] were there.

So quietly, we had some old bucket, you know, with holes, with everything rusted out-- who cares-- went to that little farm, dug out with dirty hands these-- these potatoes, filled them up in the bucket, and brought it.
So you had double ratio Passover.
Never forget that.
It's a matter of fact, I never forget, it was already bad when I was still home, in the '39 or '40 Passover.
See, the sawmill, what we had, they-- they had to watch there is no sabotage or anything.
There were soldiers there.
They had rations, all kind of rations.
They had oil-- good oil.
So I walk maybe five kilometers or so, and I said-- I watch what I did, a big boy.
The guys knew me.
I just bring that in.
That's not Mogilev.
That's still in Vatra Dorna.
Just remember that.

And I-- the soldiers, you know, give them a couple dollars-- not dollars, whatever it was, [NON-ENGLISH]-- and you brought home a bucket of oil like you would bring, now, home a bucket of diamonds-- a bucket of oil.
And the whole family-- you know, a bucket of oil?
Everybody can do their stuff.
So you improvise.
You do a lot of things the best you can, but you can pay with your life.
And then they schlepped me.
They found all the younger kids.
They put in a transport and send them, again, in a labor camp to Nikolaev from Mogilev.
Very simple-- they knew 99% where every Jew is hiding, young, old, or indifferent.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So they got the young people, with apology, again.
When I say apology, not by the Romanian or by the German-- the so-called quote, unquote, 'the Jewish community.' They got orders over there, you know?
The Germans came in, we want 300 youngsters, right now, on the train, present, right there.
So they knew.
Everybody knew something, especially, yeah?
So they came, and they grabbed-- my father was already dead.
We are talking, now, 1943, you know?
So my father was in '41.
So-- and they took me, and my mother, again, cried.

It was-- it didn't help her much crying or schlepping, because we knew the people, because of Clippers and the Bronsteins she never knew, and said, my son-- said, so everybody, you know, I could see my son was no different.
Your son, my son-- we want these boys out there.
If not, you know what's happened.
We have machine guns.
We know what is going the rest of that, right?
So they put you in a van.
Again, my mother, may she rest in peace, I don't know where she got it, must, again, give away whatever she had or so-- a half a bread.
And she run to the-- to the cattle and brought me a half a bread, because I didn't have no food, nothing, before they closed the-- and the train start going.
Crying, but didn't help her crying and begging also, because didn't mean.
There was-- as a matter of fact, in the Holocaust Museum, when I was, they show pictures in the ghettos.
They don't show pictures of Mogilev, because I don't think there were many survivors of Mogilev, because I was saw them all.
And so, for kids, youngsters, and everything, stay on every corner in the ghettos and begging, please give me, you know, whatever you get-- a piece of apple, a piece of bread.
I don't know, just beg and hungry, you know?
Already-- they're already half-dead, and half-crippled, and everything else.
But this is exactly how it was also in the ghetto over there.
And some-- we had a girl from Bessarabia, from Kishinev.
Her name was Greenberg.
She was a nurse.
She had a brother, a younger brother.
I'll never forget.
We took them both in.
They lost their parents.
The Romanian-- a lot of them were massacred.
[INAUDIBLE] So we took them in.
That guy, I don't where he is now.
Maybe they're in Israel.
But anyway, her name was Paulya.
I remember this brother-- She was about my age.
He was a young kid.
We were all young.
But well, he was all the time hungry.
Now, all you can do is suck your thumb if you're hungry.
You can dream all night about something, but it didn't help much.
Well, I was taken with all the youngster to Nikolaev.
This is called Nikolaev on the Bug.
Bug, B-U-G-- this is a big river there, an industrial city strictly occupied that was already-- not Romanian-- German city.
Bug, industrial city.
Not Bug-- Bug is a-- Nikolaev-- Nikolaev is the equivalent of industrial city.
In those day, you can say Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
You know, Pittsburgh-- big.
And there, where we were taken for plain labor.
They put us in barracks, below, like, you know, in little barracks, what you call where you put potatoes, you know, with that one roof.
You cannot lift your head.
And they have two shelters, one on top the other.
You lay next to each other, you keep warm?
And in the morning, before it gets light, you're marching out-- no clothes also, whatever.
You're marching out to work, whatever is there.
Most of the time, heavy snow, so there was no-- the big tractors, like, you have here, shovel to get the snow.
You were the tractors-- hundreds and hundreds of youngsters with shovel make the snow so the tanks can pass, right?
So they-- and they gave you in the morning [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 100 gram, they call it, of bread, and a little dish of soup.
If I may, I'd like to describe that.
The bread was made out of red kernels, which, if you ever saw a straw broom, they have red kernels-- real, not the nylon brooms which you use-- the real straw out of red kernels.
That was grinded.
As soon as you got the bread, hot bread, it got green.
They call it [NON-ENGLISH].
That's 100% poison.
And the soup was made what they call a potato soup-- but these little-- with sand and, you know, and stones from the farm.
You know, they don't sell that.
You know, the chicken pick it up on the farm, and the pigs-- that was the soup.
So if you were dumb enough-- of course, hungry enough you were.
There's no question-- but if you were stupid enough to figure that that will help you survive, and you eat it or drink, in two days, you were dead in the latrine, thrown in.
There was a trench, what's called latrine, with everything in it.
And that is your burial place with everything.
You have to take it and throw in the latrine, because you got, right away, dysentery and everything-- poison.
Just it was maybe two days a slower death then you take green poison, it maybe takes 10 minutes if there is, or you see Dr.
Kevorkian, and he can do a better job on you.
OK.
They took us over the river, over the water, in a-- in a very low boat-- low boat.
And then, if you picked up-- and I'd like to mention, if you ever picked up a ration, a can, what the Germans used to have maybe butter in there also, and if they saw you, they kill you.
They don't want you to have any kind of utensil.
But you were lucky enough, [INAUDIBLE] you got it, then you go in the water.
First, you have about-- that water, and some little fish are swimming in-- into the-- into the can.
And it seems like these little fish also with that water did not infect you with any-- it seems.
I mean, the guys who went on the little boat-- but that didn't last long, because, all of a sudden, we heard rumors the SS-- there was a SS detachment there-- need volunteer.
We didn't know for what.
They don't report.
Volunteer-- it's near a marketplace, where the farmers are.
So let's go.
What do we have to lose?
So we live a day longer, or we live a day less.
We also had the Russian boys there.
Now, the Russian boys, because they spoke already, at that time, fluent Russian-- I learned it later on-- they knew, again, some of the farmers in the thing.
And some-- of course, some were caught.
They were hanged.
But they were going through the barbed wires, at night, out to the farmers.
And when they talked to the farmer, they got something to eat, and they brought them in-- the Jewish boys, very far in-between.
But anyway, through these rumors from these Russian Jewish boys, we found out.
So we volunteered.
And we went.
So they took us to cemeteries-- non-Jewish cemeteries.
They were Christian cemeteries.
And they gave us sledgehammers.
Now, if you lift up a sledgehammer, and you don't eat nothing, first, you go-- when you hit something, you go with the sledgehammer down, because it pulls you.
The sledgehammers were, I would say, 8, 10 kilos.
They were heavy.
[INAUDIBLE]
Like, you-- you try like you want the cement-- you want to-- no, now, you break cement natural with automatic machine.
But those, they are sledgehammer-- you hit.
So we had to break up the cemetery, the stones.
And the right at the cemetery, there was highways, where the German's tanks were supposed, but they were sinking because the German-- the Russian highway was not so perfect like our highways here.
So they were all mud.
So then we had to take, break, destroy these stones, and take shovels, and put them on the street, so the tanks had to go.
While we were doing that, there were two ways.
Some of the Christians who had-- I assume they had [INAUDIBLE] came to the cemetery, and they knew what's going on, sure.
They saw-- and crossed themselves for the last time, said a prayer in Russian, and left a boiled egg or a piece of bread.
It was the last show.
I remember like today, a woman.
She walked out with a baby buggy.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah, the big ones, you know, the old-time baby buggies you know, with the spring, you know, those big ones-- black.
What do you think?
She start walking.
Now, the soldiers who watched us, they saw.
That was by the cemetery.
You know, on this side was a cemetery, there was a street, and there was small little houses, where people live, you know, the Ukraine.
So they-- she walked with the baby buggy, and guess what she had in there?
She had good-- some kind of, what the Russian make out of potato, pastry, and hot tea.
And she was walking by, and the soldier looked the other way.
And you know what that means for survival if you can get a little hot tea with this Russian potato?
What is it?
It's like a potato cake, or potato bread.
[INAUDIBLE].
And beside, the farmers who used to go to the farm, they also used to lose some eggs and bread.
When I say lose, in purpose, and we pick it up.
And that we took back in.
So we helped some of the kids over there.
